card.intuition: How Postgres handles concurrent access using Multi-Version Concurrency Control — instead of locking rows, it creates 'versions' of rows so readers don't block writers.
card.analogy: Editing a collaborative document where every time you hit save, it creates a new draft (v1, v2, v3). People can keep reading the old 'v2' while you are busy writing 'v3' in the background.
when_to_use: Optimizing database performance for high-concurrency read/write workloads
Understanding why 'VACUUM' is necessary to clean up old row versions (bloat)
Designing complex reporting queries that shouldn't slow down live traffic
gotchas: MVCC creates 'tombstones' for deleted or updated rows, leading to 'table bloat' if VACUUM isn't running frequently
Large transactions that stay open too long prevent VACUUM from cleaning up obsolete rows

steps.annotation: System overview — step through to trace the flow.
SQL string received by parser — creates parse tree.
Planner analyses stats and chooses cheapest execution plan (seq vs index scan).
Executor runs the plan: fetches pages, applies filters, sorts.
Buffer pool caches hot pages. WAL ensures durability before commit.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the name of the background process that reclaims storage space occupied by dead tuples in PostgreSQL.
exercise.starter_code: To prevent table bloat, PostgreSQL periodically runs the ______ command.
hints: It sounds like a household cleaning tool
exercise.test_code:
def check(ans): return ans.upper() == "VACUUM"
assert check("VACUUM")
print("PASS")
